notes: Chapter argument reconstruction: Ch.20 opens with the historicist prophetic scaffold (Daniel sealed until 'the time of the end,' Paul's 'man of sin' = papacy holding power 1260 years, ending 1798, therefore Daniel 'unsealed' and a judgment-hour message becoming possible only after that date). It then makes its real evidentiary move in paragraph 6 by asserting that, like the sixteenth-century Reformation, the nineteenth-century Advent Movement broke out 'in different countries... at the same time' and that isolated bodies of Christians reached the advent conviction 'solely by the study of the Scriptures... independently' -- this sentence is the thesis-statement for the whole chapter, and everything that follows (Wolff, Lacunza, Bengel, Gaussen, the Scandinavian child-preachers, then Miller and the American Millerite culmination) is offered as a catalogue of confirming instances of that independent, worldwide, providentially-patterned convergence. The chapter's persuasive weight therefore rests less on any single biographical vignette than on the cumulative pattern-claim of simultaneous independent discovery, which is used rhetorically to elevate the advent awakening (and by extension 1844) above a merely social/contagious movement into a providentially-designed one, paralleling the Reformation and priming the reader for the sanctuary/investigative-judgment material in following chapters. Individual portraits (Wolff's travels, Lacunza's Jesuit background, Bengel's Württemberg ministry, Gaussen's child-catechesis) are supporting color/texture, richly detailed but not individually load-bearing; only the 'independent convergence' framing and the Great-Disappointment/providential-test narrative at chapter's end carry real argumentative freight toward the book's Millerite-to-1844 thesis chain. No web research performed at this stage; both items assessed by re-reading the defended verdict, rationale, and full chapter context (paragraphs 1-49).

GC-20-063.reasoning: Isolated name-substitution: GC gives 'Rabbi Ben-Israel' for the pseudonym Lacunza actually used, 'Juan Josafat Ben-Ezra' -- every surrounding fact (Jesuit identity, converted-Jew disguise, motive of escaping Roman censure) is correct, and even the era's own sources (Wellcome 1874, Irving's 1827 translation title) render the name correctly as 'Ben-Ezra,' so this is White's own slip rather than an inherited period error, notable as the first contradicted verdict to survive defense in the whole project. Counterfactual: correcting or deleting the erroneous name changes nothing about the Lacunza episode's role in the chapter's diffusion catalogue -- the claim that a Jesuit reached advent convictions and published under a converted-Jew persona to avoid censure stands untouched by which name is attached to that persona. Severity reflects a genuine, avoidable factual/quotation error confined to a single proper noun; centrality is low because the specific spelling of the pseudonym carries no argumentative weight of its own within either the chapter's or the book's thesis chain.

GC-20-016.reasoning: This sentence is the interpretive keystone of paragraph 6, explicitly modeled on the Reformation parallel ('Like the great Reformation... appeared in different countries... at the same time'), and it frames every subsequent vignette (Wolff, Lacunza, Bengel, Gaussen, the Scandinavian child-preachers) as independent, Scripture-only convergence rather than ordinary transatlantic diffusion. Modern historiography documents real cross-pollination -- Irving's 1827 translation of Lacunza circulating in Britain, American Millerite publications reprinted in England, Wolff's own itinerant preaching seeding multiple regions -- so 'solely by the study of the Scriptures... independently' overstates the case, though some genuinely prior/independent study did occur (Bengel's 1740s date-setting predates any American influence entirely), which is why the verdict is disputed rather than contradicted. Counterfactual: strip the 'independence' framing and the chapter's roster of national awakenings still stands as an accurate record of a real transatlantic revival, but the chapter's specific interpretive claim -- that the ubiquity constitutes evidence of providential rather than social causation -- loses its evidentiary anchor, since ordinary diffusion is a sufficient naturalistic explanation for the same facts; this makes the claim load-bearing for the chapter's own argument (though not fatal to the book's broader thesis chain, since a diffusion-based worldwide awakening would still satisfy Revelation 14's 'to every nation' imagery).
